9. Model diagnostics

MATH 4210, Chapter 9

Leer en espanol

In 1973 two biologists, Michael Johnson and Peter Raven, tallied the number of plant species on 30 islands of the Galapagos archipelago and recorded a handful of geographic facts about each island: its area, its highest elevation, how far it sits from the nearest island, how far from the central island of Santa Cruz, and the area of the closest neighbor. The natural question is whether geography predicts biological richness. Bigger, taller, more isolated islands might hold more species. A multiple regression of species count on those five predictors is the obvious first move, and it looks like a success: it explains about 77 percent of the variation in species counts.

Then you plot the residuals, and the model falls apart. Figure 1 shows the residuals against the fitted counts. Three problems jump out at once. The spread of the residuals grows as the fitted count grows, a funnel that breaks the constant-variance assumption. One island, Isabela, sits far from the crowd. And for several small islands the model predicts a negative number of species, which is impossible. A fit that reports a high R2R^2R2 can still be wrong in every way that matters.

This chapter is about the fourth stage of the modeling workflow, CHECK. You have learned to ASK a question, EXPLORE the data, and FIT a model in matrix form (7.1 The model and least squares in matrix form) with all its inference machinery (8.4 The general linear test). Fitting is now the easy part; any computer will do it, and so will any student. The scarce skill is judgment: deciding whether a fitted model can be trusted, finding the observations that are quietly running the show, and naming exactly which assumption a model breaks. That is what diagnostics do. By the end of the chapter you will have a toolkit that turns “the fit looks fine” into a checklist you can defend.

We work two real datasets side by side. The Galapagos species data (gala) is the model that fails every test. The cross-country savings data (savings), which you first met in the correlation chapter, is the model whose assumptions mostly hold but whose fit is steered by a single country. Along the way the Toluca production data returns one last time to answer a question we could not settle before: is a straight line really the right shape?

This lesson at a glance

  • What we are doing: Checking a fitted model with leverage values, the family of studentized residuals, influence measures (Cook’s distance, DFFITS, DFBETAS), diagnostic plots and formal tests, and the lack-of-fit F test, worked on the gala and savings fits with the Toluca line returning to settle whether a straight line was ever the right shape.

  • Why we are doing it: A model can post a high R2R^2R2 and still be wrong in every way that matters; the Galapagos fit even predicts negative species counts. Fitting is easy, and judging whether a fit can be trusted is the scarce skill.

  • Main objective: Compute and read leverage values hiih_{ii}hii​, studentized deleted residuals, and influence measures, and name exactly which assumption a model breaks and which case is quietly running the fit.

  • What changed from the last chapters: Chapters 7 and 8 fit and interpreted a model as if every assumption held; this is the CHECK stage that tests them, reusing the hat matrix of 7.3 The hat matrix and the general linear test of 8.4 The general linear test (which returns disguised as the lack-of-fit test).

Learning objectives

By the end of this chapter you will be able to:

  • Derive the properties of the leverage values hiih_{ii}hii​ from the hat matrix, and compute them by hand in simple regression.

  • Distinguish the raw, semistudentized, standardized, and studentized deleted residuals, and explain why the raw residual is a misleading scale.

  • Test a single case for outlyingness with the studentized deleted residual and a Bonferroni cutoff.

  • Derive Cook’s distance from the leave-one-out coefficient change, and compute DFFITS and DFBETAS.

  • Separate a high-leverage point from an influential one, and diagnose the savings and gala fits by name.

  • Read residual, quantile-quantile, and scale-location plots, and run the Breusch-Pagan, Brown-Forsythe, Shapiro-Wilk, and Durbin-Watson tests.

  • Derive and apply the lack-of-fit F test with pure error from replicated predictor values.

Everything in this chapter answers one of two plain questions. Are any single data points trouble, either sitting in a strange spot or quietly steering the fit? Or does the model break a promise it made about the errors taken as a whole? Figure 2 is the map. The left branch chases troublesome cases (Sections 9.1 to 9.3); the right branch checks the assumptions (Sections 9.4 to 9.6); every box names the tool and the section that answers it. Come back to this picture whenever you lose the thread.

9.1 Leverage values and the hat matrix¶

Intuition¶

Before we can judge a residual we have to know how much freedom the model gave each point. Picture the fitted line as a see-saw. A point sitting near the middle of the predictor values can move up or down quite a bit without shifting the line much; the crowd around it holds the line in place. A point way out at the edge of the predictor range is different: because it is the only observation out there, the line has to follow it, so the fitted line at that point is pulled toward the point’s own response. That pull is measured by the leverage value hiih_{ii}hii​ (Definition 9.1). A high-leverage point is one whose predictor values are unusual, so the fit is forced to pay it special attention regardless of its response.

The leverage value is a statement about the predictors only. It says nothing yet about whether a point is fit well or badly. It measures potential, the capacity to influence the line, and we will spend the next two sections turning potential into a verdict.

Formula¶

Recall the hat matrix from 7.3 The hat matrix. Stacking the observations into the design matrix X\mathbf{X}X (an n×pn \times pn×p matrix whose first column is ones, with ppp the number of parameters including the intercept), the fitted values are Y^=HY\hat{\mathbf{Y}} = \mathbf{H}\mathbf{Y}Y^=HY where

H=X(X′X)−1X′.\mathbf{H} = \mathbf{X}(\mathbf{X}'\mathbf{X})^{-1}\mathbf{X}' .H=X(X′X)−1X′.

  • H\mathbf{H}H is the n×nn \times nn×n hat matrix, the orthogonal projection onto the column space of X\mathbf{X}X; it “puts the hat on Y\mathbf{Y}Y.”

  • X′\mathbf{X}'X′ is the transpose of X\mathbf{X}X, and (X′X)−1(\mathbf{X}'\mathbf{X})^{-1}(X′X)−1 is the inverse built in 6.4 The inverse and the normal equations.

Definition 9.1: Leverage value

The leverage value of case iii is the iii-th diagonal entry of the hat matrix H\mathbf{H}H,

hii=xi′(X′X)−1xi,h_{ii} = \mathbf{x}_i'(\mathbf{X}'\mathbf{X})^{-1}\mathbf{x}_i,hii​=xi′​(X′X)−1xi​,

where xi′\mathbf{x}_i'xi′​ is the iii-th row of X\mathbf{X}X (the predictors for case iii, with a leading 1). It is a weighted squared distance of case iii’s predictors from the center of all the predictors, large exactly when case iii sits in a lonely part of predictor space.

Derivation (properties of the leverage values)¶

Theorem 9.2: Properties of the leverage values

Under the linear model with hat matrix H=X(X′X)−1X′\mathbf{H} = \mathbf{X}(\mathbf{X}'\mathbf{X})^{-1}\mathbf{X}'H=X(X′X)−1X′, the leverage values satisfy

0≤hii≤1,∑i=1nhii=tr⁡(H)=p,0 \le h_{ii} \le 1, \qquad \sum_{i=1}^n h_{ii} = \operatorname{tr}(\mathbf{H}) = p,0≤hii​≤1,i=1∑n​hii​=tr(H)=p,

so their average is p/np/np/n. A common rule of thumb flags any case with hii>2p/nh_{ii} > 2p/nhii​>2p/n as a high-leverage case worth a second look.

Proof. In 7.3 The hat matrix we proved that H\mathbf{H}H is symmetric (H′=H\mathbf{H}' = \mathbf{H}H′=H) and idempotent (HH=H\mathbf{H}\mathbf{H} = \mathbf{H}HH=H), with trace equal to ppp. Three facts follow directly.

First, because H\mathbf{H}H is symmetric and idempotent, H=HH\mathbf{H} = \mathbf{H}\mathbf{H}H=HH read on the diagonal gives

hii=∑j=1nhijhji=∑j=1nhij2=hii2+∑j≠ihij2.h_{ii} = \sum_{j=1}^n h_{ij} h_{ji} = \sum_{j=1}^n h_{ij}^2 = h_{ii}^2 + \sum_{j \neq i} h_{ij}^2 .hii​=j=1∑n​hij​hji​=j=1∑n​hij2​=hii2​+j=i∑​hij2​.

Rearranging, hii−hii2=∑j≠ihij2≥0h_{ii} - h_{ii}^2 = \sum_{j \neq i} h_{ij}^2 \ge 0hii​−hii2​=∑j=i​hij2​≥0, so hii(1−hii)≥0h_{ii}(1 - h_{ii}) \ge 0hii​(1−hii​)≥0. Since hii=∑jhij2≥0h_{ii} = \sum_j h_{ij}^2 \ge 0hii​=∑j​hij2​≥0 as well, we get

0≤hii≤1.0 \le h_{ii} \le 1 .0≤hii​≤1.

In words: a leverage value is trapped between 0 and 1, and a value near 1 means the fit at that point is determined almost entirely by that point’s own response.

Second, the leverage values add up to the trace of H\mathbf{H}H:

∑i=1nhii=tr⁡(H)=p.\sum_{i=1}^n h_{ii} = \operatorname{tr}(\mathbf{H}) = p .i=1∑n​hii​=tr(H)=p.

Third, dividing by nnn, the average leverage value is p/np/np/n. A point is not carrying its fair share of predictor space if its leverage value is well above that average, which motivates the common rule of thumb: examine any case with

hii>2pn.h_{ii} > \frac{2p}{n} .hii​>n2p​.

The factor of 2 is a convention, not a law; it flags roughly the points worth a second look. ■\blacksquare■

For simple regression the leverage value has a clean closed form that shows plainly what it measures.

Theorem 9.3: Leverage values in simple regression

For simple linear regression with a single predictor, the leverage value of case iii is

hii=1n+(Xi−Xˉ)2Sxx,Sxx=∑i(Xi−Xˉ)2.h_{ii} = \frac{1}{n} + \frac{(X_i - \bar X)^2}{S_{xx}}, \qquad S_{xx} = \sum_i (X_i - \bar X)^2 .hii​=n1​+Sxx​(Xi​−Xˉ)2​,Sxx​=i∑​(Xi​−Xˉ)2.

It is a baseline 1/n1/n1/n shared by every case plus a penalty that grows with the squared distance of XiX_iXi​ from the mean predictor value.

Proof. With one predictor, xi′=(1,Xi)\mathbf{x}_i' = (1, X_i)xi′​=(1,Xi​) and, as computed in 7.3 The hat matrix,

(X′X)−1=1nSxx(∑Xi2−∑Xi−∑Xin),Sxx=∑(Xi−Xˉ)2.(\mathbf{X}'\mathbf{X})^{-1} = \frac{1}{n S_{xx}} \begin{pmatrix} \sum X_i^2 & -\sum X_i \\ -\sum X_i & n \end{pmatrix}, \qquad S_{xx} = \sum (X_i - \bar X)^2 .(X′X)−1=nSxx​1​(∑Xi2​−∑Xi​​−∑Xi​n​),Sxx​=∑(Xi​−Xˉ)2.

Multiplying out the quadratic form xi′(X′X)−1xi\mathbf{x}_i'(\mathbf{X}'\mathbf{X})^{-1}\mathbf{x}_ixi′​(X′X)−1xi​ and simplifying with ∑Xi=nXˉ\sum X_i = n\bar X∑Xi​=nXˉ gives

hii=1n+(Xi−Xˉ)2Sxx.h_{ii} = \frac{1}{n} + \frac{(X_i - \bar X)^2}{S_{xx}} .hii​=n1​+Sxx​(Xi​−Xˉ)2​.

In words: the leverage value in simple regression is a baseline 1/n1/n1/n shared by everyone, plus a penalty that grows with the squared distance of XiX_iXi​ from the mean predictor value. Figure 3 draws this parabola on the Toluca lot sizes. ■\blacksquare■

R and Python¶

Example 9.1: Leverage values in the Galapagos model

Question. Which Galapagos islands sit in an unusual part of predictor space, and do any exceed the 2p/n2p/n2p/n cutoff?

Intuition. Fit the five-predictor model, pull the diagonal of the hat matrix, and compare the largest values to 2p/n2p/n2p/n. The two biggest islands should stand out.

Formula. hii=xi′(X′X)−1xih_{ii} = \mathbf{x}_i'(\mathbf{X}'\mathbf{X})^{-1}\mathbf{x}_ihii​=xi′​(X′X)−1xi​, with cutoff 2p/n2p/n2p/n and ∑ihii=p\sum_i h_{ii} = p∑i​hii​=p as an arithmetic check.

Computation.

gala <- read.csv("data/gala.csv")
gfit <- lm(Species ~ Area + Elevation + Nearest + Scruz + Adjacent, data = gala)
round(coef(gfit), 4)
c(R2 = summary(gfit)$r.squared, sigma = summary(gfit)$sigma)
range(fitted(gfit))
sum(fitted(gfit) < 0)
(Intercept)        Area   Elevation     Nearest       Scruz    Adjacent
     7.0682     -0.0239      0.3195      0.0091     -0.2405     -0.0748
        R2      sigma
 0.7658469 60.9751884
[1] -36.38392 386.40356
[1] 5
p <- length(coef(gfit))   # 6 parameters including intercept
n <- nrow(gala)           # 30 islands
h <- hatvalues(gfit)
names(h) <- gala$island
c(p = p, n = n, cutoff_2p_over_n = 2 * p / n, sum_h = sum(h))
round(sort(h, decreasing = TRUE)[1:4], 4)
               p                n cutoff_2p_over_n            sum_h
             6.0             30.0              0.4              6.0
   Isabela Fernandina     Darwin   Genovesa
    0.9685     0.9497     0.4655     0.4301
import numpy as np
import pandas as pd
import statsmodels.formula.api as smf

gala = pd.read_csv("data/gala.csv")
gfit = smf.ols("Species ~ Area + Elevation + Nearest + Scruz + Adjacent",
               data=gala).fit()
print(gfit.params.round(4))
print("R2 =", round(gfit.rsquared, 4), " sigma =", round(np.sqrt(gfit.scale), 4))
print("fitted range:", gfit.fittedvalues.min().round(2), gfit.fittedvalues.max().round(2))
print("negative fitted:", int((gfit.fittedvalues < 0).sum()))
Intercept    7.0682
Area        -0.0239
Elevation    0.3195
Nearest      0.0091
Scruz       -0.2405
Adjacent    -0.0748
dtype: float64
R2 = 0.7658  sigma = 60.9752
fitted range: -36.38 386.4
negative fitted: 5
infl = gfit.get_influence()
h = infl.hat_matrix_diag
p, n = 6, len(gala)
print("p =", p, " n =", n, " 2p/n =", 2 * p / n, " sum h =", round(h.sum(), 4))
top = pd.Series(h, index=gala["island"]).sort_values(ascending=False)
print(top.head(4).round(4))
p = 6  n = 30  2p/n = 0.4  sum h = 6.0
island
Isabela       0.9685
Fernandina    0.9497
Darwin        0.4655
Genovesa      0.4301
dtype: float64

Interpretation. The leverage values sum to p=6p = 6p=6, as they must. Two islands blow past the 2p/n=0.402p/n = 0.402p/n=0.40 cutoff: Isabela at h=0.9685h = 0.9685h=0.9685 and Fernandina at h=0.9497h = 0.9497h=0.9497. Isabela is the largest island in the archipelago by a wide margin, so its predictor row is unlike any other, and the fit is nearly pinned to Isabela’s own species count there. That is potential to distort. Whether Isabela actually distorts the fit is a separate question, taken up in 9.3 Influence: which points actually change the fit.

An index plot makes the two standouts obvious. Figure 4 plots each island’s leverage value against its position in the data, with the 2p/n2p/n2p/n line drawn in: Isabela and Fernandina tower over the other 28 islands, and everything else sits in a harmless band.

The closed form hii=1/n+(Xi−Xˉ)2/Sxxh_{ii} = 1/n + (X_i - \bar X)^2/S_{xx}hii​=1/n+(Xi​−Xˉ)2/Sxx​ is worth checking against the software once, so you trust that hatvalues and the hat matrix agree.

toluca <- read.csv("data/toluca.csv")
tfit <- lm(hours ~ lotsize, data = toluca)
x <- toluca$lotsize
h_closed <- 1 / nrow(toluca) + (x - mean(x))^2 / sum((x - mean(x))^2)
max(abs(hatvalues(tfit) - h_closed))
[1] 2.775558e-17
toluca = pd.read_csv("data/toluca.csv")
tfit = smf.ols("hours ~ lotsize", data=toluca).fit()
x = toluca["lotsize"].to_numpy()
h_closed = 1 / len(toluca) + (x - x.mean()) ** 2 / np.sum((x - x.mean()) ** 2)
print(np.max(np.abs(tfit.get_influence().hat_matrix_diag - h_closed)))
4.163336342344337e-17

The two agree to floating-point dust, confirming the formula.

Try it 9.1

In a simple regression with n=25n = 25n=25 and Xˉ=70\bar X = 70Xˉ=70, a new run is made at a lot size of 120 with Sxx=19800S_{xx} = 19800Sxx​=19800. Compute its leverage value from the closed form, and say whether it exceeds the 2p/n2p/n2p/n cutoff (here p=2p = 2p=2).

Solution

h=1/25+(120−70)2/19800=0.04+2500/19800=0.04+0.1263=0.1663h = 1/25 + (120 - 70)^2 / 19800 = 0.04 + 2500/19800 = 0.04 + 0.1263 = 0.1663h=1/25+(120−70)2/19800=0.04+2500/19800=0.04+0.1263=0.1663. The cutoff is 2p/n=4/25=0.162p/n = 4/25 = 0.162p/n=4/25=0.16. The value 0.1663 is just above 0.16, so this run would be flagged for a look, which makes sense: at 120 it sits at the very edge of the observed lot sizes.

9.2 Four flavors of residual¶

Intuition¶

The leverage value measured which points had the power to distort the fit. The next question is which points the fit actually misses. For that we read the residual ei=Yi−Y^ie_i = Y_i - \hat Y_iei​=Yi​−Y^i​, the model’s leftover: the part of YiY_iYi​ the fit did not capture. It is tempting to hunt for outliers by scanning the raw residuals for big values. That is a trap, because raw residuals are not on a level playing field. A high-leverage point drags the line toward itself, which shrinks its own residual. So the very points most able to distort the fit tend to have deceptively small raw residuals. To compare residuals fairly we have to put them on a common scale, and that means dividing by the right standard deviation, which depends on the leverage value.

Formula¶

Definition 9.4: The four residual scales

For case iii with residual ei=Yi−Y^ie_i = Y_i - \hat Y_iei​=Yi​−Y^i​, leverage value hiih_{ii}hii​, and s=MSEs = \sqrt{\mathrm{MSE}}s=MSE​, the four residual scales, from crudest to sharpest, are

ei,eis,ri=eis1−hii,ti=eis(i)1−hii.e_i, \qquad \frac{e_i}{s}, \qquad r_i = \frac{e_i}{s\sqrt{1 - h_{ii}}}, \qquad t_i = \frac{e_i}{s_{(i)}\sqrt{1 - h_{ii}}} .ei​,sei​​,ri​=s1−hii​​ei​​,ti​=s(i)​1−hii​​ei​​.

  • eie_iei​ is the raw residual; s=MSEs = \sqrt{\mathrm{MSE}}s=MSE​ is the estimated error standard deviation.

  • ei/se_i/sei​/s is the semistudentized residual: crude, because it ignores that different residuals have different variances.

  • rir_iri​ is the standardized (internally studentized) residual, dividing by the actual estimated standard deviation of eie_iei​.

  • tit_iti​ is the studentized deleted (externally studentized) residual, where s(i)s_{(i)}s(i)​ is the error standard deviation estimated from the fit with case iii removed.

The scale factor 1−hii\sqrt{1 - h_{ii}}1−hii​​ is not decoration. It comes from an exact variance formula for the residuals.

Derivation (the variance of a residual)¶

Theorem 9.5: Variance of a residual

Under the linear model with Var⁡{Y}=σ2I\operatorname{Var}\{\mathbf{Y}\} = \sigma^2\mathbf{I}Var{Y}=σ2I, the residual vector e=(I−H)Y\mathbf{e} = (\mathbf{I} - \mathbf{H})\mathbf{Y}e=(I−H)Y has

Var⁡{e}=σ2(I−H),Var⁡{ei}=σ2(1−hii),Cov⁡{ei,ej}=−σ2hij.\operatorname{Var}\{\mathbf{e}\} = \sigma^2(\mathbf{I} - \mathbf{H}), \qquad \operatorname{Var}\{e_i\} = \sigma^2(1 - h_{ii}), \qquad \operatorname{Cov}\{e_i, e_j\} = -\sigma^2 h_{ij}.Var{e}=σ2(I−H),Var{ei​}=σ2(1−hii​),Cov{ei​,ej​}=−σ2hij​.

The residual at a high-leverage point has smaller variance, which is why dividing by 1−hii\sqrt{1 - h_{ii}}1−hii​​ is needed to restore a common scale.

Proof. Write the residual vector as a linear map of the responses. Since Y^=HY\hat{\mathbf{Y}} = \mathbf{H}\mathbf{Y}Y^=HY,

e=Y−Y^=(I−H)Y.\mathbf{e} = \mathbf{Y} - \hat{\mathbf{Y}} = (\mathbf{I} - \mathbf{H})\mathbf{Y}.e=Y−Y^=(I−H)Y.

Under the model Var⁡{Y}=σ2I\operatorname{Var}\{\mathbf{Y}\} = \sigma^2 \mathbf{I}Var{Y}=σ2I (the errors are uncorrelated with common variance, from 6.7 Random vectors, expectation, and covariance matrices). The matrix I−H\mathbf{I} - \mathbf{H}I−H is symmetric and idempotent, just like H\mathbf{H}H. Applying the covariance rule for a linear transform,

Var⁡{e}=(I−H)σ2I(I−H)′=σ2(I−H)(I−H)=σ2(I−H).\operatorname{Var}\{\mathbf{e}\} = (\mathbf{I} - \mathbf{H})\sigma^2\mathbf{I}(\mathbf{I} - \mathbf{H})' = \sigma^2 (\mathbf{I} - \mathbf{H})(\mathbf{I} - \mathbf{H}) = \sigma^2 (\mathbf{I} - \mathbf{H}).Var{e}=(I−H)σ2I(I−H)′=σ2(I−H)(I−H)=σ2(I−H).

Reading the iii-th diagonal entry,

Var⁡{ei}=σ2(1−hii),Cov⁡{ei,ej}=−σ2hij.\operatorname{Var}\{e_i\} = \sigma^2 (1 - h_{ii}), \qquad \operatorname{Cov}\{e_i, e_j\} = -\sigma^2 h_{ij}.Var{ei​}=σ2(1−hii​),Cov{ei​,ej​}=−σ2hij​.

In words: the residual at a high-leverage point has smaller variance, because the fit bends to meet it. That is exactly why a raw residual understates trouble at high-leverage points, and why dividing by 1−hii\sqrt{1 - h_{ii}}1−hii​​ restores a common scale. Figure 5 shows the shrinkage and its correction. ■\blacksquare■

Derivation (the studentized deleted residual and the outlier test)¶

The standardized residual rir_iri​ has a subtle flaw: if case iii really is an outlier, it inflates sss, the very quantity in its own denominator, masking itself. The fix is to estimate the error standard deviation from the other cases. Let s(i)s_{(i)}s(i)​ be the root mean square error of the fit with case iii deleted. A useful algebraic identity, which avoids refitting nnn times, is

(n−p−1) s(i)2=(n−p) MSE−ei21−hii.(n - p - 1)\,s_{(i)}^2 = (n - p)\,\mathrm{MSE} - \frac{e_i^2}{1 - h_{ii}} .(n−p−1)s(i)2​=(n−p)MSE−1−hii​ei2​​.

In words: this builds the leave-one-out error variance from quantities the full fit already gives you, by subtracting case iii’s own squared-error contribution, so you never actually refit.

Theorem 9.6: Distribution of the studentized deleted residual

Under the linear model, when case iii is not an outlier, the studentized deleted residual follows a ttt distribution with n−p−1n - p - 1n−p−1 degrees of freedom:

ti=eis(i)1−hii  ∼  t(n−p−1).t_i = \frac{e_i}{s_{(i)}\sqrt{1 - h_{ii}}} \;\sim\; t(n - p - 1).ti​=s(i)​1−hii​​ei​​∼t(n−p−1).

The most extreme case is flagged as an outlier when ∣ti∣>t(1−α/(2n); n−p−1)|t_i| > t(1 - \alpha/(2n);\, n - p - 1)∣ti​∣>t(1−α/(2n);n−p−1), a Bonferroni cutoff that holds the family-wise false-alarm rate below α\alphaα.

Proof. Consider the deleted residual di=Yi−Y^i(i)d_i = Y_i - \hat Y_{i(i)}di​=Yi​−Y^i(i)​, the gap between YiY_iYi​ and the prediction at xi\mathbf{x}_ixi​ from a model that never saw case iii. Because that prediction does not use YiY_iYi​, the two are independent. An identity we prove from the leave-one-out update in 9.3 Influence: which points actually change the fit gives di=ei/(1−hii)d_i = e_i / (1 - h_{ii})di​=ei​/(1−hii​), with variance

Var⁡{di}=σ21−hii.\operatorname{Var}\{d_i\} = \frac{\sigma^2}{1 - h_{ii}} .Var{di​}=1−hii​σ2​.

Estimating σ2\sigma^2σ2 by the independent s(i)2s_{(i)}^2s(i)2​ and dividing did_idi​ by its estimated standard error yields

ti=dis(i)/1−hii=eis(i)1−hii  ∼  t(n−p−1)t_i = \frac{d_i}{s_{(i)}/\sqrt{1 - h_{ii}}} = \frac{e_i}{s_{(i)}\sqrt{1 - h_{ii}}} \;\sim\; t(n - p - 1)ti​=s(i)​/1−hii​​di​​=s(i)​1−hii​​ei​​∼t(n−p−1)

under the model, when case iii is not an outlier. Because numerator and denominator are independent, this is an exact ttt ratio. ■\blacksquare■

To test whether the most extreme case is a genuine outlier you look at max⁡i∣ti∣\max_i |t_i|maxi​∣ti​∣. Since you are implicitly running nnn tests, use a Bonferroni cutoff: flag case iii when ∣ti∣>t(1−α/(2n); n−p−1)|t_i| > t(1 - \alpha/(2n);\, n - p - 1)∣ti​∣>t(1−α/(2n);n−p−1). The correction keeps the chance of a single false alarm across all nnn cases below α\alphaα.

Example 9.2: Ranking the savings residuals four ways

Question. In the savings model, how do Zambia and Chile look on each residual scale, and is either a significant outlier?

Intuition. Fit the model, compute all four residual scales for the two most extreme countries, then run the Bonferroni outlier test on the whole set.

Formula. eie_iei​, ei/se_i/sei​/s, rir_iri​, tit_iti​ as above, with cutoff t(1−α/(2n); n−p−1)t(1 - \alpha/(2n);\, n - p - 1)t(1−α/(2n);n−p−1).

Computation.

savings <- read.csv("data/savings.csv")
sfit <- lm(sr ~ pop15 + pop75 + dpi + ddpi, data = savings)
s <- summary(sfit)$sigma
e <- residuals(sfit)
hs <- hatvalues(sfit)
i <- which(savings$country %in% c("Zambia", "Chile"))
data.frame(country = savings$country[i],
           hii = round(hs[i], 3),
           raw_e = round(e[i], 3),
           semistud = round(e[i] / s, 3),
           standardized = round(rstandard(sfit)[i], 3),
           stud_deleted = round(rstudent(sfit)[i], 3))
   country   hii  raw_e semistud standardized stud_deleted
7    Chile 0.037 -8.242   -2.167       -2.209       -2.313
46  Zambia 0.064  9.751    2.564        2.651        2.854
rstud <- rstudent(gfit)
cutoff <- qt(1 - 0.05 / (2 * n), n - p - 1)
c(most_extreme = rstud[which.max(abs(rstud))], bonferroni_cutoff = cutoff)
gala$island[which.max(abs(rstud))]
  most_extreme.16 bonferroni_cutoff
        -5.333694          3.559678
[1] "Isabela"
savings = pd.read_csv("data/savings.csv")
sfit = smf.ols("sr ~ pop15 + pop75 + dpi + ddpi", data=savings).fit()
si = sfit.get_influence()
s = np.sqrt(sfit.scale)
e = sfit.resid.to_numpy()
tab = pd.DataFrame({
    "country": savings["country"],
    "hii": si.hat_matrix_diag.round(3),
    "raw_e": e.round(3),
    "semistud": (e / s).round(3),
    "standardized": si.resid_studentized_internal.round(3),
    "stud_deleted": si.resid_studentized_external.round(3),
})
print(tab[savings["country"].isin(["Zambia", "Chile"])].to_string(index=False))
country   hii  raw_e  semistud  standardized  stud_deleted
  Chile 0.037 -8.242    -2.167        -2.209        -2.313
 Zambia 0.064  9.751     2.564         2.651         2.854
from scipy import stats

gi = gfit.get_influence()
rstud = gi.resid_studentized_external
cutoff = stats.t.ppf(1 - 0.05 / (2 * n), n - p - 1)
j = int(np.argmax(np.abs(rstud)))
print("most extreme:", round(rstud[j], 4), " island:", gala["island"].iloc[j])
print("Bonferroni cutoff:", round(cutoff, 4))
most extreme: -5.3337  island: Isabela
Bonferroni cutoff: 3.5597

Interpretation. For Zambia the four scales climb from 2.56 to 2.85 as we sharpen the denominator: the studentizing correction matters. Zambia’s studentized deleted residual is 2.85, and Chile’s is -2.31, both notable but neither past the savings Bonferroni cutoff of about 3.53 (for n=50n = 50n=50, p=5p = 5p=5). In the Galapagos model, though, Isabela’s studentized deleted residual is -5.33, far beyond that model’s cutoff of 3.56 (for n=30n = 30n=30, p=6p = 6p=6): Isabela is a genuine statistical outlier, not just an unusual predictor row.

Durable skill: Put comparisons on a fair scale

The raw residual looks like the obvious thing to compare, and it is the wrong thing, because different points had different room to deviate. Standardizing by the correct standard deviation is a move you will make everywhere: comparing test scores across classes, returns across assets, defect rates across factories of different sizes. Whenever you rank raw numbers, stop and ask whether the units of “surprise” are the same for every item. If they are not, divide by the right yardstick first.

9.3 Influence: which points actually change the fit¶

Intuition¶

A high leverage value is potential and a large residual is a symptom; influence is the thing we actually care about, the amount the fit would change if a point were gone (Definition 9.7). A point can be high-leverage yet have little influence, if it happens to fall right on the trend the other points set. And a point with a large residual but an ordinary leverage value may tug the line only a little. Influence is the product of the two ingredients: an unusual predictor row and a response the model fits poorly.

Figure 6 makes the point with four small pictures. In each one we drop a single red point into the same cloud and refit. Push the point far out in XXX but keep it on the trend, and the line does not budge: a high leverage value, no influence. Give it a big vertical miss but an ordinary XXX, and the line barely tilts: a plain outlier. Only when the point is both far out in XXX and off the trend does the line swing to chase it. That last panel is influence, and it takes both ingredients at once.

Definition 9.7: Influential observation

An influential observation is a case whose removal would noticeably change the fitted model, that is the fitted values or the estimated coefficients. Influence combines two ingredients: an unusual predictor row (a high leverage value) and a response the model fits poorly (a large residual).

A common misreading

A student looks at the savings residuals and says, “Zambia has the biggest residual, so Zambia is the point most distorting the fit.” That confuses a large residual with influence. Zambia has an ordinary leverage value (h=0.064h = 0.064h=0.064), so its big residual moves the fit only modestly. Libya, with a middling residual but the highest leverage value in the data, is the country that actually bends the model. The right question is never “who is fit worst?” but “who would change the answer if they left?”

Formula¶

Definition 9.8: Cook’s distance

Cook’s distance measures the influence of case iii by how far all the fitted values move when the case is deleted:

Di=∑j=1n(Y^j−Y^j(i))2p MSE=ri2p⋅hii1−hii,D_i = \frac{\sum_{j=1}^n (\hat Y_j - \hat Y_{j(i)})^2}{p\,\mathrm{MSE}} = \frac{r_i^2}{p}\cdot\frac{h_{ii}}{1 - h_{ii}} ,Di​=pMSE∑j=1n​(Y^j​−Y^j(i)​)2​=pri2​​⋅1−hii​hii​​,

where Y^j(i)\hat Y_{j(i)}Y^j(i)​ is the fitted value for case jjj from the model refit without case iii, rir_iri​ is the standardized residual, and hiih_{ii}hii​ the leverage value of case iii.

The second form makes the recipe explicit: influence equals a residual part (ri2r_i^2ri2​) times a factor hii/(1−hii)h_{ii}/(1 - h_{ii})hii​/(1−hii​) that grows with the leverage value. Both must be sizable for DiD_iDi​ to be large. A rough guide treats DiD_iDi​ near or above 1 as worth serious attention, and any case far above the rest as worth naming.

Definition 9.9: DFFITS and DFBETAS

Two companion measures localize influence. DFFITS rescales the change in a single fitted value, and DFBETAS rescales the change in a single coefficient:

DFFITSi=tihii1−hii,DFBETASk(i)=bk−bk(i)s(i)(X′X)kk−1.\mathrm{DFFITS}_i = t_i\sqrt{\frac{h_{ii}}{1 - h_{ii}}}, \qquad \mathrm{DFBETAS}_{k(i)} = \frac{b_k - b_{k(i)}}{s_{(i)}\sqrt{(\mathbf{X}'\mathbf{X})^{-1}_{kk}}} .DFFITSi​=ti​1−hii​hii​​​,DFBETASk(i)​=s(i)​(X′X)kk−1​​bk​−bk(i)​​.

Common cutoffs are ∣DFFITSi∣>2p/n|\mathrm{DFFITS}_i| > 2\sqrt{p/n}∣DFFITSi​∣>2p/n​ and ∣DFBETASk(i)∣>2/n|\mathrm{DFBETAS}_{k(i)}| > 2/\sqrt{n}∣DFBETASk(i)​∣>2/n​.

Key idea

The leverage value and the residual are not rivals but factors. A point moves the fit only when it is both unusual in its predictors (a high leverage value) and poorly fit in its response (a large residual); either ingredient alone leaves the line largely undisturbed. Every influence measure in this chapter combines the leverage value hiih_{ii}hii​ with the size of the residual.

Derivation (the leave-one-out update and Cook’s distance)¶

Every influence measure rests on one algebraic fact: you can compute the fit without case iii from the full fit, no refitting required.

Theorem 9.10: Leave-one-out coefficient update

Deleting case iii from the least-squares fit shifts the coefficient vector by

b−b(i)=(X′X)−1xi ei1−hii,\mathbf{b} - \mathbf{b}_{(i)} = \frac{(\mathbf{X}'\mathbf{X})^{-1}\mathbf{x}_i\, e_i}{1 - h_{ii}},b−b(i)​=1−hii​(X′X)−1xi​ei​​,

so the fit without case iii can be recovered from the full fit alone, with no refitting required.

Proof. Deleting case iii removes the row xi′\mathbf{x}_i'xi′​ from X\mathbf{X}X and the value YiY_iYi​ from Y\mathbf{Y}Y, so

X(i)′X(i)=X′X−xixi′,X(i)′Y(i)=X′Y−xiYi.\mathbf{X}_{(i)}'\mathbf{X}_{(i)} = \mathbf{X}'\mathbf{X} - \mathbf{x}_i\mathbf{x}_i', \qquad \mathbf{X}_{(i)}'\mathbf{Y}_{(i)} = \mathbf{X}'\mathbf{Y} - \mathbf{x}_i Y_i .X(i)′​X(i)​=X′X−xi​xi′​,X(i)′​Y(i)​=X′Y−xi​Yi​.

The Sherman-Morrison formula gives the inverse of a rank-one downdate: for an invertible A\mathbf{A}A and vector u\mathbf{u}u with u′A−1u≠1\mathbf{u}'\mathbf{A}^{-1}\mathbf{u} \neq 1u′A−1u=1,

(A−uu′)−1=A−1+A−1uu′A−11−u′A−1u,(\mathbf{A} - \mathbf{u}\mathbf{u}')^{-1} = \mathbf{A}^{-1} + \frac{\mathbf{A}^{-1}\mathbf{u}\mathbf{u}'\mathbf{A}^{-1}}{1 - \mathbf{u}'\mathbf{A}^{-1}\mathbf{u}} ,(A−uu′)−1=A−1+1−u′A−1uA−1uu′A−1​,

which you can verify by multiplying the right side by A−uu′\mathbf{A} - \mathbf{u}\mathbf{u}'A−uu′ and collecting terms to get I\mathbf{I}I. Set A=X′X\mathbf{A} = \mathbf{X}'\mathbf{X}A=X′X and u=xi\mathbf{u} = \mathbf{x}_iu=xi​, so that u′A−1u=hii\mathbf{u}'\mathbf{A}^{-1}\mathbf{u} = h_{ii}u′A−1u=hii​. Multiplying the updated inverse by X(i)′Y(i)\mathbf{X}_{(i)}'\mathbf{Y}_{(i)}X(i)′​Y(i)​ and simplifying (using xi′b=Y^i\mathbf{x}_i'\mathbf{b} = \hat Y_ixi′​b=Y^i​ and ei=Yi−Y^ie_i = Y_i - \hat Y_iei​=Yi​−Y^i​) collapses to the compact result

b−b(i)=(X′X)−1xi ei1−hii.\mathbf{b} - \mathbf{b}_{(i)} = \frac{(\mathbf{X}'\mathbf{X})^{-1}\mathbf{x}_i\, e_i}{1 - h_{ii}} .b−b(i)​=1−hii​(X′X)−1xi​ei​​.

In words: dropping case iii shifts the coefficient vector by a multiple of (X′X)−1xi(\mathbf{X}'\mathbf{X})^{-1}\mathbf{x}_i(X′X)−1xi​, scaled by the case’s residual and inflated by 1/(1−hii)1/(1 - h_{ii})1/(1−hii​) when the leverage value is high. Setting the shift’s effect at xi\mathbf{x}_ixi​ itself gives Y^i−Y^i(i)=hiiei/(1−hii)\hat Y_i - \hat Y_{i(i)} = h_{ii} e_i/(1 - h_{ii})Y^i​−Y^i(i)​=hii​ei​/(1−hii​), so the deleted residual is di=Yi−Y^i(i)=ei+hiiei/(1−hii)=ei/(1−hii)d_i = Y_i - \hat Y_{i(i)} = e_i + h_{ii}e_i/(1-h_{ii}) = e_i/(1 - h_{ii})di​=Yi​−Y^i(i)​=ei​+hii​ei​/(1−hii​)=ei​/(1−hii​), the identity promised in 9.2 Four flavors of residual. ■\blacksquare■

Theorem 9.11: Cook’s distance identity

Cook’s distance admits the closed form

Di=ei2 hiip MSE (1−hii)2=ri2p⋅hii1−hii,D_i = \frac{e_i^2\, h_{ii}}{p\,\mathrm{MSE}\,(1 - h_{ii})^2} = \frac{r_i^2}{p}\cdot\frac{h_{ii}}{1 - h_{ii}},Di​=pMSE(1−hii​)2ei2​hii​​=pri2​​⋅1−hii​hii​​,

splitting influence into a squared standardized residual ri2r_i^2ri2​ and a factor hii/(1−hii)h_{ii}/(1 - h_{ii})hii​/(1−hii​) built from the leverage value; both must be sizable for DiD_iDi​ to be large.

Proof. The numerator of DiD_iDi​ is a quadratic form in that shift, because Y^−Y^(i)=X(b−b(i))\hat{\mathbf{Y}} - \hat{\mathbf{Y}}_{(i)} = \mathbf{X}(\mathbf{b} - \mathbf{b}_{(i)})Y^−Y^(i)​=X(b−b(i)​):

∑j(Y^j−Y^j(i))2=(b−b(i))′X′X(b−b(i)).\sum_j (\hat Y_j - \hat Y_{j(i)})^2 = (\mathbf{b} - \mathbf{b}_{(i)})'\mathbf{X}'\mathbf{X}(\mathbf{b} - \mathbf{b}_{(i)}).j∑​(Y^j​−Y^j(i)​)2=(b−b(i)​)′X′X(b−b(i)​).

Substitute the deletion formula. The middle X′X\mathbf{X}'\mathbf{X}X′X cancels one inverse on each side, leaving xi′(X′X)−1xi=hii\mathbf{x}_i'(\mathbf{X}'\mathbf{X})^{-1}\mathbf{x}_i = h_{ii}xi′​(X′X)−1xi​=hii​:

(b−b(i))′X′X(b−b(i))=ei2(1−hii)2 xi′(X′X)−1xi=ei2 hii(1−hii)2.(\mathbf{b} - \mathbf{b}_{(i)})'\mathbf{X}'\mathbf{X}(\mathbf{b} - \mathbf{b}_{(i)}) = \frac{e_i^2}{(1 - h_{ii})^2}\,\mathbf{x}_i'(\mathbf{X}'\mathbf{X})^{-1}\mathbf{x}_i = \frac{e_i^2\, h_{ii}}{(1 - h_{ii})^2} .(b−b(i)​)′X′X(b−b(i)​)=(1−hii​)2ei2​​xi′​(X′X)−1xi​=(1−hii​)2ei2​hii​​.

Divide by p MSEp\,\mathrm{MSE}pMSE and recognize ri2=ei2/(MSE(1−hii))r_i^2 = e_i^2/(\mathrm{MSE}(1 - h_{ii}))ri2​=ei2​/(MSE(1−hii​)):

Di=ei2 hiip MSE (1−hii)2=ri2p⋅hii1−hii.D_i = \frac{e_i^2\, h_{ii}}{p\,\mathrm{MSE}\,(1 - h_{ii})^2} = \frac{r_i^2}{p}\cdot\frac{h_{ii}}{1 - h_{ii}} .Di​=pMSE(1−hii​)2ei2​hii​​=pri2​​⋅1−hii​hii​​.

This is the promised split of DiD_iDi​ into a squared standardized residual and a term in the leverage value hiih_{ii}hii​. ■\blacksquare■

R and Python¶

Example 9.3: Cook’s distance for the savings and gala fits

Question. Which country most influences the savings fit, and how does the Galapagos fit compare?

Intuition. Compute Cook’s distance for every case, check the closed form against the built-in values, and name the largest.

Formula. Di=(ri2/p) hii/(1−hii)D_i = (r_i^2/p)\,h_{ii}/(1 - h_{ii})Di​=(ri2​/p)hii​/(1−hii​).

Computation.

D <- cooks.distance(sfit)
round(sort(D, decreasing = TRUE)[1:4], 4)
r <- rstandard(sfit)
D_check <- r^2 / length(coef(sfit)) * hs / (1 - hs)
max(abs(D - D_check))
round(sort(cooks.distance(gfit), decreasing = TRUE)[1:3], 3)
gala$island[which.max(cooks.distance(gfit))]
    49     23     46     21
0.2681 0.1428 0.0966 0.0544
[1] 5.551115e-17
    16     25     12
68.076  0.394  0.268
[1] "Isabela"
si = sfit.get_influence()
D = si.cooks_distance[0]
order = np.argsort(D)[::-1]
print(pd.Series(D[order][:4].round(4), index=savings["country"].iloc[order][:4]))
r = si.resid_studentized_internal
hs = si.hat_matrix_diag
D_check = r ** 2 / 5 * hs / (1 - hs)
print("max |D - identity|:", np.max(np.abs(D - D_check)))
Dg = gi.cooks_distance[0]
og = np.argsort(Dg)[::-1]
print(pd.Series(Dg[og][:3].round(3), index=gala["island"].iloc[og][:3]))
country
Libya      0.2681
Japan      0.1428
Zambia     0.0966
Ireland    0.0544
dtype: float64
max |D - identity|: 2.7755575615628914e-17
island
Isabela       68.076
SantaCruz      0.394
Fernandina     0.268

Interpretation. The closed form matches the built-in Cook’s distance exactly. In the savings data Libya leads at D=0.27D = 0.27D=0.27, ahead of Japan and Zambia; no country reaches the alarm level of 1, but Libya clearly stands apart, as Figure 7 shows. The Galapagos story is on another scale entirely: Isabela’s Cook’s distance is 68.1, roughly 170 times the next island’s. Isabela is not merely a high-leverage point, it is enormously influential, the single case most responsible for the model’s failure.

The influence plot in Figure 8 combines the whole story: the leverage value on one axis, studentized deleted residual on the other, and Cook’s distance as bubble size. It separates the roles that a one-number summary blends together.

Naming an influential point is not the same as deleting it. The honest workflow is to refit without the case and report how much the answer moves, so a reader can judge. Figure 9 and the numbers behind it do exactly that.

Example 9.4: What Libya does to the growth coefficient

Question. How much does Libya move the estimated effect of income growth (ddpi) on the savings rate?

Intuition. DFBETAS scales the coefficient change; then refit without Libya and read the ddpi coefficient directly.

Formula. DFFITSi=tihii/(1−hii)\mathrm{DFFITS}_i = t_i\sqrt{h_{ii}/(1 - h_{ii})}DFFITSi​=ti​hii​/(1−hii​)​ and DFBETASk(i)\mathrm{DFBETAS}_{k(i)}DFBETASk(i)​ as above, with the coefficient table from a refit.

Computation.

lib <- which(savings$country == "Libya")
c(dffits_Libya = dffits(sfit)[lib],
  dffits_cutoff = 2 * sqrt(length(coef(sfit)) / nrow(savings)),
  dfbetas_ddpi_Libya = dfbetas(sfit)[lib, "ddpi"])
sfit_noLibya <- lm(sr ~ pop15 + pop75 + dpi + ddpi, data = savings[-lib, ])
rbind(all = coef(sfit), no_Libya = coef(sfit_noLibya))
   dffits_Libya.49      dffits_cutoff dfbetas_ddpi_Libya
        -1.1601334          0.6324555         -1.0244773
         (Intercept)      pop15     pop75           dpi      ddpi
all         28.56609 -0.4611931 -1.691498 -0.0003369019 0.4096949
no_Libya    24.52405 -0.3914401 -1.280867 -0.0003189001 0.6102790
lib = int(np.where(savings["country"] == "Libya")[0][0])
print("DFFITS Libya:", round(si.dffits[0][lib], 4),
      " cutoff:", round(2 * np.sqrt(5 / 50), 4),
      " DFBETAS ddpi:", round(si.dfbetas[lib, 4], 4))
sfit_noLibya = smf.ols("sr ~ pop15 + pop75 + dpi + ddpi",
                       data=savings.drop(index=lib)).fit()
print(pd.DataFrame({"all": sfit.params, "no_Libya": sfit_noLibya.params}).round(4))
DFFITS Libya: -1.1601  cutoff: 0.6325  DFBETAS ddpi: -1.0245
               all  no_Libya
Intercept  28.5661   24.5240
pop15      -0.4612   -0.3914
pop75      -1.6915   -1.2809
dpi        -0.0003   -0.0003
ddpi        0.4097    0.6103

Interpretation. Libya’s DFFITS is -1.16, well past the 0.63 cutoff, and its DFBETAS for ddpi is -1.02, meaning Libya alone shifts the growth coefficient by more than one standard error. Refitting confirms it: the ddpi coefficient jumps from 0.41 to 0.61, a rise of about a half when one country leaves. Libya had an unusually high income growth rate paired with a modest savings rate, and because its leverage value is so high, it drags the growth slope down. Any conclusion about how growth affects saving hangs on a single data point, which is precisely the kind of fragility diagnostics exist to expose.

Try it 9.2

A point has leverage value hii=0.5h_{ii} = 0.5hii​=0.5 and standardized residual ri=2r_i = 2ri​=2 in a model with p=4p = 4p=4 parameters. Compute its Cook’s distance. Then say what happens to DiD_iDi​ if the leverage value rises to 0.9 with the same residual.

Solution

Di=(ri2/p) hii/(1−hii)=(4/4)(0.5/0.5)=1.0D_i = (r_i^2/p)\,h_{ii}/(1 - h_{ii}) = (4/4)(0.5/0.5) = 1.0Di​=(ri2​/p)hii​/(1−hii​)=(4/4)(0.5/0.5)=1.0. Raising the leverage value to 0.9: Di=(4/4)(0.9/0.1)=9.0D_i = (4/4)(0.9/0.1) = 9.0Di​=(4/4)(0.9/0.1)=9.0. The factor h/(1−h)h/(1-h)h/(1−h) explodes as h→1h \to 1h→1, so the same residual becomes far more influential at a high leverage value. Influence is the residual and the leverage value working together, not either alone.

9.4 Reading the diagnostic plots¶

Intuition¶

Formal numbers are sharp, but the fastest way to catch a broken model is to look at it. Three plots do most of the work, and each targets one assumption. The residual-versus-fitted plot checks the shape of the mean function and the constancy of variance. The normal quantile-quantile plot checks the shape of the error distribution. The scale-location plot checks constant variance again, more sensitively. You learned to draw the first of these back in 2.3 Fitted values and the properties of residuals; now you can read all three as a set.

Figure 10 is a field guide to the residual-versus-fitted plot. Four shapes recur: a formless horizontal band (healthy), a funnel (variance grows with the mean), a bend (the mean function is the wrong shape), and a lone stray point (an outlier). Train your eye on these and most diagnostics become pattern recognition.

R and Python¶

You have already seen the Galapagos residual-versus-fitted plot in Figure 1: a textbook funnel. The normal quantile-quantile plot in Figure 11 tells the normality half of the story. In R it is one line, qqnorm(residuals(gfit)); qqline(residuals(gfit)), and Python’s statsmodels offers sm.qqplot(gfit.resid, line="s"). If the errors were normal the points would hug the reference line; here the upper tail bends away and Isabela plunges far below, the visual echo of its -5.33 studentized residual.

The scale-location plot in Figure 12 plots ∣ri∣\sqrt{|r_i|}∣ri​∣​ against the fitted value. A flat cloud means constant variance; a rising trend means the spread grows with the mean. The Galapagos cloud climbs steadily, confirming the funnel from a second angle.

Try it 9.3

You fit a model and the residual-versus-fitted plot shows a clear U shape, low in the middle and high at both ends. Which assumption is violated, and is transforming YYY or adding a term the likely remedy?

Solution

A U shape is a bend: the mean function is the wrong shape, not a variance problem. The straight-line model is missing curvature. The likely remedy is to add a quadratic term in the predictor (or transform XXX), not to transform YYY, which mainly addresses nonconstant variance. Chapter 10 and Chapter 11 develop both fixes.

9.5 Formal tests for the assumptions¶

Intuition¶

Plots persuade; tests put a number on the suspicion. Use them together, and lean on the plots when the two disagree, because a test can miss a pattern a plot makes obvious, and a test can flag a wrinkle too small to matter. There is one test for each assumption: constant variance, normal errors, and independence.

Key idea

A formal test and a diagnostic plot answer the same question with different strengths. On a small sample a test can lack the power to confirm what a plot shows plainly; on a large sample it can reject a departure too small to matter. When the picture and the p-value disagree, trust the picture and let the test sharpen, not overrule, your judgment.

For constant variance, two tests are standard. The Breusch-Pagan test regresses the squared residuals on the predictors and asks whether that regression explains anything; if the spread depends on the predictors, it does. The Brown-Forsythe test splits the data into groups, compares the spread of residuals across groups using medians (so it resists outliers), and is a good choice when you suspect variance changes with one particular predictor.

For normality, the Shapiro-Wilk test compares the ordered residuals to what normal data would produce; a small p-value says the residuals are not normal. A close relative is the correlation test for normality, which computes the correlation between the ordered residuals and their normal scores (the points in the quantile-quantile plot) and rejects normality when that correlation is too far below 1.

For independence in time-ordered data, the Durbin-Watson test checks whether consecutive errors are correlated.

Formula¶

The Durbin-Watson statistic is

D=∑t=2n(et−et−1)2∑t=1net2≈2(1−ρ^),D = \frac{\sum_{t=2}^n (e_t - e_{t-1})^2}{\sum_{t=1}^n e_t^2} \approx 2(1 - \hat\rho),D=∑t=1n​et2​∑t=2n​(et​−et−1​)2​≈2(1−ρ^​),

where ρ^\hat\rhoρ^​ is the estimated lag-1 autocorrelation of the residuals, the correlation between each residual and the one before it in time order.

  • DDD ranges from 0 to 4. D≈2D \approx 2D≈2 means no autocorrelation (ρ^≈0\hat\rho \approx 0ρ^​≈0).

  • D<2D < 2D<2 signals positive autocorrelation (errors drift in runs, ρ^>0\hat\rho > 0ρ^​>0).

  • D>2D > 2D>2 signals negative autocorrelation (errors alternate in sign).

In words: Durbin-Watson compares how much neighboring residuals differ to how big the residuals are overall; when neighbors are alike, the numerator is small and DDD drops below 2. The exact null distribution depends on the design matrix, so classic tables give two bounds dLd_LdL​ and dUd_UdU​; modern software returns an exact p-value instead. This diagnostic is the entry point for 15.2 Why time breaks ordinary least squares, where time-ordered errors are the whole subject and where models are built that fix the problem rather than just detect it.

R and Python¶

Example 9.5: Assumption tests on both models

Question. Do the constant-variance and normality tests confirm what the plots suggested, that the Galapagos model is broken and the savings model is not?

Intuition. Run Breusch-Pagan and Shapiro-Wilk on both fits and compare p-values.

Formula. Breusch-Pagan (regress squared residuals on predictors) and Shapiro-Wilk (ordered residuals versus normal scores).

Computation.

library(lmtest)
bptest(gfit)          # Galapagos
bptest(sfit)          # savings
shapiro.test(residuals(gfit))
shapiro.test(residuals(sfit))
	studentized Breusch-Pagan test
data:  gfit
BP = 9.7959, df = 5, p-value = 0.08123

	studentized Breusch-Pagan test
data:  sfit
BP = 4.9852, df = 4, p-value = 0.2888

	Shapiro-Wilk normality test
data:  residuals(gfit)
W = 0.91351, p-value = 0.01826

	Shapiro-Wilk normality test
data:  residuals(sfit)
W = 0.98698, p-value = 0.8524
import statsmodels.stats.api as sms

bp_g = sms.het_breuschpagan(gfit.resid, gfit.model.exog)
bp_s = sms.het_breuschpagan(sfit.resid, sfit.model.exog)
sh_g = stats.shapiro(gfit.resid)
sh_s = stats.shapiro(sfit.resid)
print(f"gala BP:    stat={bp_g[0]:.4f}  p={bp_g[1]:.4f}")
print(f"savings BP: stat={bp_s[0]:.4f}  p={bp_s[1]:.4f}")
print(f"gala Shapiro:    W={sh_g[0]:.4f}  p={sh_g[1]:.4f}")
print(f"savings Shapiro: W={sh_s[0]:.4f}  p={sh_s[1]:.4f}")
gala BP:    stat=9.7959  p=0.0812
savings BP: stat=4.9852  p=0.2888
gala Shapiro:    W=0.9135  p=0.0183
savings Shapiro: W=0.9870  p=0.8524

Interpretation. The savings model passes both tests comfortably (variance p-value 0.29, normality p-value 0.85), matching the plots: its trouble is influence, not a broken assumption. The Galapagos normality test rejects (p=0.018p = 0.018p=0.018), confirming the quantile-quantile plot. Its Breusch-Pagan p-value is 0.08, not below 0.05, even though the funnel is obvious to the eye. This is the lesson about trusting plots: a formal test on n=30n = 30n=30 points can lack the power to catch heteroscedasticity that a picture makes undeniable. When the picture and the test disagree, believe the picture and dig further.

When normality fails, as it does for gala, one honest response is to stop relying on the normal-theory t and F machinery and switch to the resampling inference of 5.4 The bootstrap for regression, which does not assume a normal error shape. The deeper fix, changing the model so its errors behave, is the subject of Chapter 10 and, for count data like species totals, Chapter 14.

The Durbin-Watson test needs data in a meaningful order, so it does not really apply to the savings countries, which have no natural sequence. To see it work we simulate two time-ordered series: one with independent errors, one with errors that carry over from step to step.

Example 9.6: Durbin-Watson on ordered errors

Question. How does the Durbin-Watson statistic respond to independent versus autocorrelated errors?

Intuition. Build two series with a shared seed, one clean and one with positive carryover, and compare their statistics.

Formula. D=∑t≥2(et−et−1)2/∑tet2≈2(1−ρ^)D = \sum_{t \ge 2}(e_t - e_{t-1})^2 / \sum_t e_t^2 \approx 2(1 - \hat\rho)D=∑t≥2​(et​−et−1​)2/∑t​et2​≈2(1−ρ^​).

Computation.

set.seed(4210)
nD <- 60; tt <- seq_len(nD)
y_ind <- 5 + 0.3 * tt + rnorm(nD, 0, 3)
eps <- numeric(nD); eps[1] <- rnorm(1, 0, 3)
for (k in 2:nD) eps[k] <- 0.7 * eps[k - 1] + rnorm(1, 0, 3)
y_ar <- 5 + 0.3 * tt + eps
dwtest(lm(y_ind ~ tt))
dwtest(lm(y_ar ~ tt))
dwtest(sfit)
	Durbin-Watson test
data:  lm(y_ind ~ tt)
DW = 1.8909, p-value = 0.2875

	Durbin-Watson test
data:  lm(y_ar ~ tt)
DW = 0.66845, p-value = 4.99e-10

	Durbin-Watson test
data:  sfit
DW = 1.9341, p-value = 0.3897
from statsmodels.stats.stattools import durbin_watson

rng = np.random.default_rng(4210)
nD = 60
tt = np.arange(1, nD + 1)
y_ind = 5 + 0.3 * tt + rng.normal(0, 3, nD)
eps = np.empty(nD)
eps[0] = rng.normal(0, 3)
for k in range(1, nD):
    eps[k] = 0.7 * eps[k - 1] + rng.normal(0, 3)
y_ar = 5 + 0.3 * tt + eps
fit_ind = smf.ols("y ~ tt", data=pd.DataFrame({"y": y_ind, "tt": tt})).fit()
fit_ar = smf.ols("y ~ tt", data=pd.DataFrame({"y": y_ar, "tt": tt})).fit()
print("DW independent:", round(durbin_watson(fit_ind.resid), 4))
print("DW AR(1):      ", round(durbin_watson(fit_ar.resid), 4))
print("DW savings:    ", round(durbin_watson(sfit.resid), 4))
DW independent: 2.1282
DW AR(1):       0.8991
DW savings:     1.9341

Interpretation. With independent errors the statistic sits near 2 (R gives 1.89, Python 2.13; the two draw from different random number generators). With positive autocorrelation it drops far below 2 (R 0.67, Python 0.90) and the test rejects decisively, because consecutive errors track each other, as Figure 13 shows. The savings statistic, 1.93, is near 2, but that is not evidence of independence so much as a reminder that Durbin-Watson only makes sense when the rows have an order, which cross-country data do not.

9.6 The lack-of-fit F test¶

Intuition¶

Every diagnostic so far asks whether the errors misbehave. The lack-of-fit test asks a different question: is the shape of the regression function right at all? Suppose the true mean of YYY bends, but you fit a straight line. Then even with perfect data the line will miss the true means. The trouble is that ordinary SSE cannot tell “the model is wrong” from “the data are noisy,” because both inflate it. The way out is replication. If you have several observations at the same predictor value, the spread among them measures pure noise, with no help from any model. That gives a yardstick of irreducible error to compare the model’s misses against.

The Toluca data are built for this. The 25 runs used only 11 distinct lot sizes, so most lot sizes were repeated. Figure 14 shows the idea: at each lot size the runs scatter around their own group mean (pure error), and the group means scatter around the fitted line (lack of fit).

Formula¶

Definition 9.12: Pure error and lack-of-fit sums of squares

Let the predictor take ccc distinct values, with njn_jnj​ observations at level jjj and n=∑jnjn = \sum_j n_jn=∑j​nj​. The error sum of squares splits as

SSE⏟df n−2=SSPE⏟df n−c+SSLF⏟df c−2,SSPE=∑j=1c∑k(Yjk−Yˉj)2,SSLF=∑j=1cnj(Yˉj−Y^j)2.\underbrace{\mathrm{SSE}}_{\text{df } n-2} = \underbrace{\mathrm{SSPE}}_{\text{df } n-c} + \underbrace{\mathrm{SSLF}}_{\text{df } c-2}, \qquad \mathrm{SSPE} = \sum_{j=1}^c \sum_{k} (Y_{jk} - \bar Y_j)^2, \quad \mathrm{SSLF} = \sum_{j=1}^c n_j (\bar Y_j - \hat Y_j)^2 .df n−2SSE​​=df n−cSSPE​​+df c−2SSLF​​,SSPE=j=1∑c​k∑​(Yjk​−Yˉj​)2,SSLF=j=1∑c​nj​(Yˉj​−Y^j​)2.

SSPE\mathrm{SSPE}SSPE, the pure error sum of squares, is the spread of observations around their group means Yˉj\bar Y_jYˉj​ and needs no model; SSLF\mathrm{SSLF}SSLF, the lack-of-fit sum of squares, is the gap between the group means and the fitted line Y^j\hat Y_jY^j​.

Theorem 9.13: Lack-of-fit F test

Under the simple linear regression model with ccc distinct predictor levels (c>2c > 2c>2) and replication, the lack-of-fit statistic follows an FFF distribution:

F∗=SSLF/(c−2)SSPE/(n−c)=MSLFMSPE  ∼  F(c−2, n−c).F^\ast = \frac{\mathrm{SSLF}/(c-2)}{\mathrm{SSPE}/(n-c)} = \frac{\mathrm{MSLF}}{\mathrm{MSPE}} \;\sim\; F(c-2,\, n-c) .F∗=SSPE/(n−c)SSLF/(c−2)​=MSPEMSLF​∼F(c−2,n−c).

A large F∗F^\astF∗ says the group means stray too far from the line to blame on noise: the straight-line shape is wrong.

Derivation (lack of fit as a general linear test)¶

Proof. This is the general linear test of 8.4 The general linear test with a specific pair of models. The full model puts a free mean μj\mu_jμj​ at each of the ccc distinct predictor levels: Yjk=μj+εjkY_{jk} = \mu_j + \varepsilon_{jk}Yjk​=μj​+εjk​. Its least-squares fitted value at level jjj is the group mean Yˉj\bar Y_jYˉj​, so its error sum of squares is exactly SSPE=∑j∑k(Yjk−Yˉj)2\mathrm{SSPE} = \sum_j \sum_k (Y_{jk} - \bar Y_j)^2SSPE=∑j​∑k​(Yjk​−Yˉj​)2, with n−cn - cn−c degrees of freedom because it estimates ccc means. The reduced model is the straight line Yjk=β0+β1Xj+εjkY_{jk} = \beta_0 + \beta_1 X_j + \varepsilon_{jk}Yjk​=β0​+β1​Xj​+εjk​, whose error sum of squares is the ordinary SSE\mathrm{SSE}SSE with n−2n - 2n−2 degrees of freedom.

The general linear test statistic is

F∗=[SSE(R)−SSE(F)]/[dfR−dfF]SSE(F)/dfF=[SSE−SSPE]/[(n−2)−(n−c)]SSPE/(n−c)=SSLF/(c−2)SSPE/(n−c).F^\ast = \frac{[\mathrm{SSE}(R) - \mathrm{SSE}(F)]/[df_R - df_F]}{\mathrm{SSE}(F)/df_F} = \frac{[\mathrm{SSE} - \mathrm{SSPE}]/[(n-2)-(n-c)]}{\mathrm{SSPE}/(n-c)} = \frac{\mathrm{SSLF}/(c-2)}{\mathrm{SSPE}/(n-c)} .F∗=SSE(F)/dfF​[SSE(R)−SSE(F)]/[dfR​−dfF​]​=SSPE/(n−c)[SSE−SSPE]/[(n−2)−(n−c)]​=SSPE/(n−c)SSLF/(c−2)​.

That the difference SSE−SSPE\mathrm{SSE} - \mathrm{SSPE}SSE−SSPE equals ∑jnj(Yˉj−Y^j)2\sum_j n_j(\bar Y_j - \hat Y_j)^2∑j​nj​(Yˉj​−Y^j​)2 follows from expanding Yjk−Y^j=(Yjk−Yˉj)+(Yˉj−Y^j)Y_{jk} - \hat Y_j = (Y_{jk} - \bar Y_j) + (\bar Y_j - \hat Y_j)Yjk​−Y^j​=(Yjk​−Yˉj​)+(Yˉj​−Y^j​), squaring, and summing: the cross term vanishes because ∑k(Yjk−Yˉj)=0\sum_k (Y_{jk} - \bar Y_j) = 0∑k​(Yjk​−Yˉj​)=0 within each group. Under the reduced model the two mean squares both estimate σ2\sigma^2σ2, so F∗F^\astF∗ is near 1; when the true means depart from a line, only MSLF\mathrm{MSLF}MSLF inflates, since E{MSPE}=σ2E\{\mathrm{MSPE}\} = \sigma^2E{MSPE}=σ2 always. ■\blacksquare■

R and Python¶

Example 9.7: Is the Toluca line the right shape?

Question. Do the Toluca group means depart from a straight line by more than pure noise?

Intuition. Fit the line (reduced) and the group-means model (full), then compare their error sums of squares with the F test.

Formula. F∗=MSLF/MSPEF^\ast = \mathrm{MSLF}/\mathrm{MSPE}F∗=MSLF/MSPE on c−2c - 2c−2 and n−cn - cn−c degrees of freedom.

Computation.

reduced <- lm(hours ~ lotsize, data = toluca)
full <- lm(hours ~ factor(lotsize), data = toluca)
anova(reduced, full)
Analysis of Variance Table

Model 1: hours ~ lotsize
Model 2: hours ~ factor(lotsize)
  Res.Df   RSS Df Sum of Sq      F Pr(>F)
1     23 54825
2     14 37581  9     17245 0.7138 0.6893
reduced = smf.ols("hours ~ lotsize", data=toluca).fit()
full = smf.ols("hours ~ C(lotsize)", data=toluca).fit()
SSE_R = reduced.ssr
SSPE = full.ssr
SSLF = SSE_R - SSPE
c = full.df_model + 1          # number of distinct lot sizes
df_lf = int(c - 2)
df_pe = int(len(toluca) - c)
F = (SSLF / df_lf) / (SSPE / df_pe)
pval = stats.f.sf(F, df_lf, df_pe)
print(f"SSE={SSE_R:.1f} SSPE={SSPE:.1f} SSLF={SSLF:.1f}")
print(f"F = {F:.4f} on {df_lf} and {df_pe} df, p = {pval:.4f}")
SSE=54825.5 SSPE=37580.8 SSLF=17244.6
F = 0.7138 on 9 and 14 df, p = 0.6893

Interpretation. With 11 distinct lot sizes and 25 runs, the test has c−2=9c - 2 = 9c−2=9 and n−c=14n - c = 14n−c=14 degrees of freedom. The pure-error sum of squares is 37581 and the lack-of-fit sum of squares is 17245, giving F∗=0.71F^\ast = 0.71F∗=0.71 and a p-value of 0.69. There is no evidence of lack of fit: the straight line the Toluca engineers relied on since Chapter 2 is the right shape after all. When a lack-of-fit test passes, you have earned the right to trust the linear form; when it fails, Chapter 11’s polynomial and interaction terms are the next move.

Try it 9.4

An experiment has c=5c = 5c=5 distinct doses, n=20n = 20n=20 animals, SSPE=300\mathrm{SSPE} = 300SSPE=300, and SSLF=450\mathrm{SSLF} = 450SSLF=450. Compute F∗F^\astF∗ and its degrees of freedom. Roughly, is lack of fit a concern (the upper 5 percent point of F(3,15)F(3, 15)F(3,15) is about 3.29)?

Solution

Degrees of freedom: c−2=3c - 2 = 3c−2=3 and n−c=15n - c = 15n−c=15. MSLF=450/3=150\mathrm{MSLF} = 450/3 = 150MSLF=450/3=150, MSPE=300/15=20\mathrm{MSPE} = 300/15 = 20MSPE=300/15=20, so F∗=150/20=7.5F^\ast = 150/20 = 7.5F∗=150/20=7.5. Since 7.5>3.297.5 > 3.297.5>3.29, reject: there is significant lack of fit, and the straight line is the wrong shape for these doses.

Durable skill: Separate signal you can reduce from noise you cannot

The lack-of-fit test works by finding a floor of irreducible error (the spread of repeats) and asking whether the model’s misses sit above that floor. That split, into error the model could remove and error nothing could, is one of the most transferable ideas in all of statistics. It is the logic behind measurement repeatability studies, the irreducible-error term in machine learning, and any honest accounting of “how good could a model possibly get?” Whenever you can repeat a condition, do, because repeats are the only direct measurement of the noise you are up against.

9.7 Chapter summary¶

Diagnostics turn “the fit looks fine” into a defensible checklist. You can now derive the leverage values from the hat matrix, bound them between 0 and 1, show they sum to ppp, and compute them by hand in simple regression; place a residual on four scales and explain why the raw residual misleads at high-leverage points; test a case for outlyingness with the studentized deleted residual and a Bonferroni cutoff; derive Cook’s distance from the leave-one-out coefficient change and read it, with DFFITS and DFBETAS, to find the points that actually move the fit; read the residual, quantile-quantile, and scale-location plots and run the Breusch-Pagan, Brown-Forsythe, Shapiro-Wilk, and Durbin-Watson tests; and derive and apply the lack-of-fit F test with pure error. The Galapagos fit failed on every count while the savings fit was steered by one country, and the Toluca line was vindicated at last.

Key results at a glance

ResultStatement or formulaValid when
Leverage value (Def 9.1)hii=xi′(X′X)−1xih_{ii} = \mathbf{x}_i'(\mathbf{X}'\mathbf{X})^{-1}\mathbf{x}_ihii​=xi′​(X′X)−1xi​any least-squares fit
Leverage values: bounds, sum (Thm 9.2)0≤hii≤1,  ∑ihii=p0 \le h_{ii} \le 1,\ \ \sum_i h_{ii} = p0≤hii​≤1,  ∑i​hii​=pany least-squares fit
Leverage value, simple regression (Thm 9.3)hii=1/n+(Xi−Xˉ)2/Sxxh_{ii} = 1/n + (X_i - \bar X)^2/S_{xx}hii​=1/n+(Xi​−Xˉ)2/Sxx​one predictor
Residual variance (Thm 9.5)Var⁡{ei}=σ2(1−hii)\operatorname{Var}\{e_i\} = \sigma^2(1 - h_{ii})Var{ei​}=σ2(1−hii​)linear model, Var⁡{Y}=σ2I\operatorname{Var}\{\mathbf{Y}\} = \sigma^2\mathbf{I}Var{Y}=σ2I
Studentized deleted residual (Thm 9.6)ti=ei/(s(i)1−hii)∼t(n−p−1)t_i = e_i/(s_{(i)}\sqrt{1 - h_{ii}}) \sim t(n-p-1)ti​=ei​/(s(i)​1−hii​​)∼t(n−p−1)case iii not an outlier
Leave-one-out update (Thm 9.10)b−b(i)=(X′X)−1xiei/(1−hii)\mathbf{b} - \mathbf{b}_{(i)} = (\mathbf{X}'\mathbf{X})^{-1}\mathbf{x}_i e_i/(1 - h_{ii})b−b(i)​=(X′X)−1xi​ei​/(1−hii​)hii<1h_{ii} < 1hii​<1
Cook’s distance (Thm 9.11)Di=(ri2/p) hii/(1−hii)D_i = (r_i^2/p)\,h_{ii}/(1 - h_{ii})Di​=(ri2​/p)hii​/(1−hii​)any least-squares fit
DFFITS, DFBETAS (Def 9.9)tihii/(1−hii)t_i\sqrt{h_{ii}/(1-h_{ii})}ti​hii​/(1−hii​)​; (bk−bk(i))/(s(i)(X′X)kk−1)(b_k - b_{k(i)})/(s_{(i)}\sqrt{(\mathbf{X}'\mathbf{X})^{-1}_{kk}})(bk​−bk(i)​)/(s(i)​(X′X)kk−1​​)per-case influence
Durbin-WatsonD=∑(et−et−1)2/∑et2≈2(1−ρ^)D = \sum(e_t - e_{t-1})^2/\sum e_t^2 \approx 2(1-\hat\rho)D=∑(et​−et−1​)2/∑et2​≈2(1−ρ^​)time-ordered errors
Lack-of-fit F (Thm 9.13)F∗=MSLF/MSPE∼F(c−2, n−c)F^\ast = \mathrm{MSLF}/\mathrm{MSPE} \sim F(c-2,\, n-c)F∗=MSLF/MSPE∼F(c−2,n−c)replicated XXX, linear mean

Key terms. leverage value, high-leverage point, raw residual, semistudentized residual, standardized residual, studentized deleted residual, deleted residual, outlier, influential observation, Cook’s distance, DFFITS, DFBETAS, Breusch-Pagan test, Brown-Forsythe test, Shapiro-Wilk test, correlation test for normality, Durbin-Watson test, autocorrelation, pure error, lack of fit.

You should now be able to

  • Derive the properties of the leverage values from the hat matrix and compute them by hand in simple regression.

  • Distinguish the raw, semistudentized, standardized, and studentized deleted residuals, and explain why the raw residual is a misleading scale.

  • Test a single case for outlyingness with the studentized deleted residual and a Bonferroni cutoff.

  • Derive Cook’s distance from the leave-one-out coefficient change, and compute DFFITS and DFBETAS.

  • Separate a high-leverage point from an influential one, and diagnose the savings and gala fits by name.

  • Read residual, quantile-quantile, and scale-location plots, and run the Breusch-Pagan, Brown-Forsythe, Shapiro-Wilk, and Durbin-Watson tests.

  • Derive and apply the lack-of-fit F test with pure error from replicated predictor values.

Where this fits. This chapter is the CHECK stage of the workflow spine from The modeling workflow: after you FIT a model you must find out whether it can be trusted before you USE it to predict or decide. Diagnostics look backward to the matrix machinery of 7.3 The hat matrix, which built the hat matrix whose diagonal holds the leverage values and whose properties power every influence measure, and to the general linear test of 8.4 The general linear test, which is the lack-of-fit test in disguise. They look forward to Chapter 10, which takes the two broken fits of this chapter, the Galapagos funnel and its non-normal errors, and tries to repair them with transformations and weighted least squares, continuing the savings and gala case studies by name. The gala thread runs further still: Chapter 14 gives the honest resolution with Poisson regression, the right tool for a count response that can never go negative. One diagnostic here is only introduced, not fully used: the Durbin-Watson statistic becomes the whole subject of 15.2 Why time breaks ordinary least squares, where time-ordered errors are modeled and corrected rather than merely detected.

9.8 Frequently asked questions¶

Q1. Is a high-leverage point a bad thing? Not by itself. A high leverage value means an unusual predictor row, which is potential to influence the fit, not proof of harm. A high-leverage point that sits right on the trend set by the others barely moves the line. A high leverage value becomes a problem only when it pairs with a large residual, and that combination is what Cook’s distance measures.

Q2. Should I delete an influential point? Almost never automatically. An influential point is a signal to investigate, not garbage to discard. Check it for a data-entry error first. If it is real, the honest report shows the fit with and without it and explains the difference, as we did with Libya. Deleting real data to get a cleaner answer is how analyses go wrong.

Q3. What is the difference between an outlier and an influential point? An outlier has a large residual: the model fits it poorly. An influential point changes the fit when removed. They can occur separately. Zambia is close to an outlier at an ordinary leverage value, so it is fit poorly but does not run the model. Libya is influential without an extreme residual, because its leverage value is so high.

Q4. My residual plot looks fine but Shapiro-Wilk rejects normality. Who wins? Read the sample size. In large samples formal tests reject tiny, harmless departures from normality; in small samples they miss real ones (the Galapagos Breusch-Pagan p-value of 0.08 is an example of the miss). Use the plot to judge whether the departure is big enough to matter, and remember that the t and F tests are fairly forgiving of mild non-normality.

Q5. Why divide by s(i)s_{(i)}s(i)​ instead of sss for the outlier test? If case iii is truly an outlier, including it inflates the ordinary sss, which sits in the denominator of rir_iri​ and hides the very case you are testing. Estimating the error standard deviation from the other cases, s(i)s_{(i)}s(i)​, removes that self-masking and gives an exact ttt distribution for tit_iti​.

Q6. Does Durbin-Watson apply to any regression? No. It tests correlation between consecutive errors, so it only means something when the rows have a real order, usually time. Running it on cross-sectional data like the savings countries is meaningless, because “consecutive” is just alphabetical order. Chapter 15 uses it where it belongs.

Q7. Cook’s distance has no p-value. How large is too large? Cook’s distance is a descriptive scale, not a formal test. Common guidance flags DiD_iDi​ near or above 1, but the most useful reading is relative: look for cases that stand far above the rest in an index plot. Libya at 0.27 is below 1 yet clearly separated from the pack, which is enough to warrant a closer look.

Q8. If a model fails a diagnostic, is it useless? No. A diagnostic tells you which assumption broke and therefore which conclusions to distrust. The Galapagos fit still describes a real association between geography and species richness; what it cannot support is a normal-theory prediction interval or a claim about a small island’s exact count. Knowing the limits of a model is what lets you use the part that holds.

9.9 Practice problems¶

Note

Unless a problem says otherwise, use the gala model Species ~ Area + Elevation + Nearest + Scruz + Adjacent (n=30n = 30n=30, p=6p = 6p=6) or the savings model sr ~ pop15 + pop75 + dpi + ddpi (n=50n = 50n=50, p=5p = 5p=5). Problems are marked (A) concepts, (B) theory, or (C) data analysis. Odd-numbered answers appear in Appendix H; full solutions are in the instructor materials.

  1. (A) In one sentence each, define the leverage value, outlier, and influential point, and say which two combine to make the third.

  2. (A) A leverage value comes out as hii=1.3h_{ii} = 1.3hii​=1.3 in someone’s homework. Explain how you know it is wrong without seeing the data.

  3. (A) Why does a high-leverage point tend to have a small raw residual? Refer to the variance formula for eie_iei​.

  4. (A) State the 2p/n2p/n2p/n rule and compute the cutoff for the savings model. Which single fact about the hat matrix makes p/np/np/n the average leverage value?

  5. (A) Explain in plain words why the studentized deleted residual uses s(i)s_{(i)}s(i)​ rather than sss.

  6. (A) Give the residual-versus-fitted pattern you would expect for (i) nonconstant variance, (ii) a missing quadratic term, (iii) a single gross outlier.

  7. (A) A colleague deletes every point with ∣ti∣>2|t_i| > 2∣ti​∣>2 and reports the cleaned fit. Give two reasons this is bad practice.

  8. (A) The savings Durbin-Watson statistic is 1.93. Explain why this is not evidence that the savings errors are independent.

  9. (A) Interpret Libya’s DFBETAS of -1.02 for the ddpi coefficient for a reader who has never seen the statistic.

  10. (B) Using the symmetry and idempotence of H\mathbf{H}H, prove that 0≤hii≤10 \le h_{ii} \le 10≤hii​≤1 and that ∑ihii=p\sum_i h_{ii} = p∑i​hii​=p (Theorem 9.2).

  11. (B) Derive the simple-regression leverage value formula hii=1/n+(Xi−Xˉ)2/Sxxh_{ii} = 1/n + (X_i - \bar X)^2/S_{xx}hii​=1/n+(Xi​−Xˉ)2/Sxx​ from hii=xi′(X′X)−1xih_{ii} = \mathbf{x}_i'(\mathbf{X}'\mathbf{X})^{-1}\mathbf{x}_ihii​=xi′​(X′X)−1xi​ (Theorem 9.3).

  12. (B) Prove Var⁡{e}=σ2(I−H)\operatorname{Var}\{\mathbf{e}\} = \sigma^2(\mathbf{I} - \mathbf{H})Var{e}=σ2(I−H), and deduce both Var⁡{ei}\operatorname{Var}\{e_i\}Var{ei​} and Cov⁡{ei,ej}\operatorname{Cov}\{e_i, e_j\}Cov{ei​,ej​} (Theorem 9.5).

  13. (B) Verify the Sherman-Morrison formula for (A−uu′)−1(\mathbf{A} - \mathbf{u}\mathbf{u}')^{-1}(A−uu′)−1 by multiplying it by A−uu′\mathbf{A} - \mathbf{u}\mathbf{u}'A−uu′ and simplifying to I\mathbf{I}I.

  14. (B) Starting from the deletion formula b−b(i)=(X′X)−1xiei/(1−hii)\mathbf{b} - \mathbf{b}_{(i)} = (\mathbf{X}'\mathbf{X})^{-1}\mathbf{x}_i e_i/(1 - h_{ii})b−b(i)​=(X′X)−1xi​ei​/(1−hii​) (Theorem 9.10), derive the closed form Di=(ri2/p) hii/(1−hii)D_i = (r_i^2/p)\,h_{ii}/(1 - h_{ii})Di​=(ri2​/p)hii​/(1−hii​) (Theorem 9.11).

  15. (B) Show that the deleted residual satisfies di=ei/(1−hii)d_i = e_i/(1 - h_{ii})di​=ei​/(1−hii​) and that Var⁡{di}=σ2/(1−hii)\operatorname{Var}\{d_i\} = \sigma^2/(1 - h_{ii})Var{di​}=σ2/(1−hii​).

  16. (B) Derive the lack-of-fit decomposition SSE=SSPE+SSLF\mathrm{SSE} = \mathrm{SSPE} + \mathrm{SSLF}SSE=SSPE+SSLF by expanding Yjk−Y^jY_{jk} - \hat Y_jYjk​−Y^j​ and showing the cross term vanishes (Theorem 9.13).

  17. (B) Explain why E{MSPE}=σ2E\{\mathrm{MSPE}\} = \sigma^2E{MSPE}=σ2 whether or not the linear model is correct, but E{MSLF}>σ2E\{\mathrm{MSLF}\} > \sigma^2E{MSLF}>σ2 when it is not. Identify the full and reduced models behind the lack-of-fit F test.

  18. (B) Show algebraically that DFFITSi=tihii/(1−hii)\mathrm{DFFITS}_i = t_i\sqrt{h_{ii}/(1 - h_{ii})}DFFITSi​=ti​hii​/(1−hii​)​ starting from the change in the single fitted value Y^i−Y^i(i)\hat Y_i - \hat Y_{i(i)}Y^i​−Y^i(i)​ and the deletion formula.

  19. (B) Using D=2(1−ρ^)D = 2(1 - \hat\rho)D=2(1−ρ^​), find the value of ρ^\hat\rhoρ^​ implied by a Durbin-Watson statistic of 0.67, and explain what that autocorrelation means for the errors.

  20. (C) Fit the gala model. Report the leverage values above 2p/n2p/n2p/n, and confirm that the leverage values sum to ppp.

  21. (C) For the savings model, compute all four residual scales for the United States and Japan, and comment on how the studentizing changes the picture for each.

  22. (C) Run the Bonferroni outlier test on the savings model. Report the most extreme studentized deleted residual, the cutoff, and your decision.

  23. (C) Compute Cook’s distance for the gala model, make an index plot, and confirm Isabela’s value with the (ri2/p) hii/(1−hii)(r_i^2/p)\,h_{ii}/(1-h_{ii})(ri2​/p)hii​/(1−hii​) formula.

  24. (C) Refit the gala model without Isabela and report how the Elevation and Area coefficients change. Comment on whether the conclusions are fragile.

  25. (C) Make the residual-versus-fitted, normal quantile-quantile, and scale-location plots for the savings model, and argue from them that its assumptions are acceptable.

  26. (C) Run the Breusch-Pagan and Shapiro-Wilk tests on the gala model, then on a model that uses log⁡(Species)\log(\text{Species})log(Species) as the response. Does the log help? (This previews Chapter 10.) Also run the Brown-Forsythe (modified Levene) test for constant variance on the raw gala model by splitting the cases at the median fitted value and running a two-sample ttt test on the absolute deviations of the residuals from their group medians; does it agree with Breusch-Pagan?

  27. (C) Using toluca.csv, run the lack-of-fit F test by hand: build the group means, compute SSPE and SSLF, form F∗F^\astF∗, and match anova.

  28. (C) Simulate a regression with positively autocorrelated errors (seed 4210, ρ=0.8\rho = 0.8ρ=0.8, n=40n = 40n=40) and one with independent errors, and compare their Durbin-Watson statistics and p-values.

  29. (B) A point has hii=0.8h_{ii} = 0.8hii​=0.8 and lies exactly on the fitted line (ei=0e_i = 0ei​=0). Compute its Cook’s distance and DFFITS, and explain why a high-leverage point can have zero influence.

  30. (C) For the gala model, produce the influence plot (leverage value versus studentized deleted residual, bubble size Cook’s distance) and identify every island that is unusual on at least one axis.

9.10 Exam practice¶

These five questions are written in the style of the course exams. Each asks you to explain your reasoning in full sentences, not just to report a number. Where a question shows software output, it was produced on the course machine from the same data/ files you used all term; you read the numbers off the printout and interpret them. Work each one before opening the model answer.

EP 9.1. The savings model sr ~ pop15 + pop75 + dpi + ddpi (n=50n = 50n=50, p=5p = 5p=5) is fit, and the residual scales for Chile and Zambia, the two countries with the largest raw residuals, are printed below along with the Bonferroni outlier cutoff.

savings <- read.csv("data/savings.csv")
sfit <- lm(sr ~ pop15 + pop75 + dpi + ddpi, data = savings)
n <- nrow(savings); p <- length(coef(sfit))
i <- which(savings$country %in% c("Chile", "Zambia"))
data.frame(country = savings$country[i],
           hii = round(hatvalues(sfit)[i], 3),
           raw_e = round(residuals(sfit)[i], 3),
           standardized = round(rstandard(sfit)[i], 3),
           stud_deleted = round(rstudent(sfit)[i], 3))
qt(1 - 0.05 / (2 * n), n - p - 1)
 country   hii  raw_e standardized stud_deleted
   Chile 0.037 -8.242       -2.209       -2.313
  Zambia 0.064  9.751        2.651        2.854
[1] 3.5258

Explain why Zambia’s residual grows from 2.564 on the semistudentized scale (its raw residual 9.751 divided by s=3.803s = 3.803s=3.803) to 2.854 once it is studentized and deleted, and say which feature of Zambia in the table drives that increase. Then state whether the outlier test flags Zambia as a genuine outlier, name the number you compare it against, and explain in one sentence what the Bonferroni correction protects you from.

Model answer

The four scales climb because each divides by a smaller, more honest yardstick. The semistudentized residual divides only by sss, treating every residual as if it had the same variance. But Theorem 9.5 says Var⁡{ei}=σ2(1−hii)\operatorname{Var}\{e_i\} = \sigma^2(1 - h_{ii})Var{ei​}=σ2(1−hii​), so Zambia’s residual, sitting at leverage value h=0.064h = 0.064h=0.064, actually has slightly less than the full error variance; dividing by s1−hiis\sqrt{1 - h_{ii}}s1−hii​​ instead of sss raises the value to the standardized 2.651. The studentized deleted residual goes one step further and estimates the error standard deviation from the other 49 countries as s(i)s_{(i)}s(i)​, which removes Zambia’s own inflation of sss and pushes the value to 2.854. The leverage value h=0.064h = 0.064h=0.064 and the self-masking of sss are the two features driving the climb.

For the outlier test you compare the largest studentized deleted residual against the Bonferroni cutoff t(1−α/(2n); n−p−1)=3.5258t(1 - \alpha/(2n);\, n - p - 1) = 3.5258t(1−α/(2n);n−p−1)=3.5258. Zambia’s 2.854 is well below 3.53, so it is not a significant outlier: it is fit poorly but not beyond what 50 draws from a clean model can produce. The Bonferroni correction divides α\alphaα by nnn because you are implicitly running one test per case; without it, scanning 50 residuals for the largest would flag a clean point roughly one time in every two datasets, and the correction holds the family-wide false-alarm rate below α\alphaα.

A weak answer just reports “2.854 < 3.53, not an outlier” without explaining that the studentizing climb comes from dividing by a smaller variance, and without saying that the Bonferroni cutoff exists precisely because you selected the most extreme of many cases.

EP 9.2. The same savings fit gives the influence summary below for its three most talked-about countries.

 country   hii rstudent cooksD dffits
   Japan 0.223    1.603  0.143  0.860
  Zambia 0.064    2.854  0.097  0.748
   Libya 0.531   -1.089  0.268 -1.160
   ( 2p/n = 0.200,   DFFITS cutoff 2*sqrt(p/n) = 0.632 )

A student writes: “Zambia has the largest studentized residual of any country, so Zambia is the single point most distorting the savings fit, and I would refit without it.” Evaluate this claim. State whether it is right, correct the reasoning using the numbers in the table, and name the country that actually most influences the fit, with the measure that settles it.

Model answer

The claim is wrong because it confuses a large residual with influence. A residual measures how badly a point is fit; influence measures how much the fit would move if the point left, and Definition 9.7 says influence needs two ingredients at once, an unusual predictor row (a high leverage value) and a poorly fit response (a large residual). Zambia has the largest studentized residual, 2.854, but its leverage value is only h=0.064h = 0.064h=0.064, close to the average p/n=0.10p/n = 0.10p/n=0.10 and far below the 2p/n=0.202p/n = 0.202p/n=0.20 cutoff. A point sitting in an ordinary part of predictor space can miss its own response badly and still barely tilt the line, because the crowd around it holds the line in place. That is why Zambia’s Cook’s distance is a modest 0.097.

Libya is the influential country. Its leverage value h=0.531h = 0.531h=0.531 is the highest in the data, and although its residual is unremarkable (t=−1.089t = -1.089t=−1.089), the product of the two ingredients is what matters: Cook’s distance Di=(ri2/p) hii/(1−hii)D_i = (r_i^2/p)\,h_{ii}/(1 - h_{ii})Di​=(ri2​/p)hii​/(1−hii​) combines them, and Libya’s 0.268 is the largest in the data, nearly triple Zambia’s. Its DFFITS of -1.160 also clears the 0.632 cutoff while Zambia’s does not. Cook’s distance is the measure that settles it. The honest next step is not to delete anyone but to refit without Libya and report how much the answer moves, as Example 9.4 does with the growth coefficient.

A weak answer simply swaps “Zambia” for “Libya” without explaining that influence is the product of the leverage value and the residual, and so misses why the biggest residual is not the biggest problem.

EP 9.3. For the Galapagos model Species ~ Area + Elevation + Nearest + Scruz + Adjacent (n=30n = 30n=30, p=6p = 6p=6), a grader runs the Breusch-Pagan and Shapiro-Wilk tests and inspects the fitted values.

gala <- read.csv("data/gala.csv")
gfit <- lm(Species ~ Area + Elevation + Nearest + Scruz + Adjacent, data = gala)
library(lmtest)
bptest(gfit)
shapiro.test(residuals(gfit))
range(fitted(gfit)); sum(fitted(gfit) < 0)
	studentized Breusch-Pagan test
data:  gfit
BP = 9.7959, df = 5, p-value = 0.08123

	Shapiro-Wilk normality test
data:  residuals(gfit)
W = 0.91351, p-value = 0.01826

[1] -36.38392 386.40356
[1] 5

A student concludes: “The Breusch-Pagan p-value is 0.081, which is above 0.05, so the constant-variance assumption holds and the Galapagos model is fine.” Evaluate this conclusion. Explain what the test does and does not show here, bring in the other evidence on the printout, and state the general rule about tests versus plots that this case illustrates.

Model answer

The conclusion is not defensible. The Breusch-Pagan p-value of 0.081 means the test fails to reject constant variance at the 5%5\%5% level, but failing to reject is not the same as confirming: it can happen because the assumption holds, or because the test lacks the power to detect a real problem. With only n=30n = 30n=30 islands the test is underpowered, and the residual-versus-fitted plot (Figure 1) shows an obvious funnel, the spread widening as the fitted count grows. When a formal test and a clear plot disagree, you trust the plot and treat the test as too weak to overrule it. So the constant-variance assumption is in fact suspect despite the p-value.
